Let $S$ denote the set of 2 by 2 matrices with integer entries and determinant
1, and let $T$ denote those matrices of $S$ which are congruent to the identity
matrix $I \bmod 3$ (so $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} \in T$
means that $a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{Z}$, $ad - bc = 1$, and 3 divides $b, c, a -
1, d - 1$).

\begin{enumerate}[(a)]

\item Let $f : T \to \mathbb{R}$ (the real numbers) be a function such that for
      every $X, Y \in T$ with $Y \ne I$, either $f(XY) > f(X)$ or $f (XY^{-1}) >
      f(X)$ (or both). Show that given two finite nonempty subsets $A, B$ of
      $T$, there are matrices $a \in A$ and $b \in B$ such that if $a' \in A$,
      $b' \in B$ and $a' b' = ab$, then $a' = a$ and $b' = b$.

\item Show that there is no $f : S \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for every $X, Y
      \in S$ with $Y \ne \pm I$, either $f (XY) > f (X)$ or $f(XY^{-1}) > f(X)$.

\end{enumerate}
